![](/wiki/File:Millet-1697117_1920.jpg)

Millet, pre-harvest

**Millets** are a general agronomic and culinary group of small-seeded cereal grains,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) widely grown around the world for food and fodder. The group includes various sub-types.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Most millets produce very small, round grains after hulling.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5) The color is light, and the flavor is mild if slightly nutty and corn-like.[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) The grain's protein content is high,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)[[7]](#cite_note-:6-7) though it is gluten-free.[[8]](#cite_note-:8-8) Many varieties of millet are drought and pest-resistant, and they are suitable for cultivation in harsh environments. Finger millet has a very high malting power.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) Most millets are difficult to mill due to the small size and the strong attachment of the bran; as a result, it is often processed into a whole-grain flour.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)

### Varieties

[edit | edit source]

There are many varieties of millet cultivated globally. Fonio and sorghum are sometimes grouped with millets, but they are often considered separate cereal grains. Several common varieties of millet are as follows:

* *Panicum miliaceum[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)*[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10): Common/hog/proso/Indian/white millet, broomcorn[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)
* *Eleusine coracana[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)*[[11]](#cite_note-:9-11): Finger/birdsfoot millet, ragi[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[11]](#cite_note-:9-11)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)
* *Pennisetum typhoides[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)*: Pearl/cattail/bulrush millet, gero, bajra[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)
* *Echinochloa frumentacea*: Japanese barnyard millet[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)
* *Setaria italica[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)*

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Millet may be sold as whole grains, cracked grains, or meal/flour.[[6]](#cite_note-:11-6) The whole millet grains store extremely well, with excellent pest resistance.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) Once ground, the flour goes rancid relatively quickly due to oils present in the whole-grain flour.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) Storing millet flour in the fridge extends its lifetime.[[12]](#cite_note-:12-12)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Millets are principally food sources in arid and semi-arid regions of the world,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10) including parts of Africa and Asia.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) The hulled grains can be cooked whole as in a porridge or pilaf or ground to a flour and used in various flatbreads.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:3-5)[[13]](#cite_note-:7-13) It can also be malted; fermented into beer or distilled into liquor;[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9) and in India, some millets can be popped like popcorn.*[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)*[[12]](#cite_note-:12-12)

A simple cooked millet dish can be made by toasting millet in a pan, adding 5 volumes of boiling water per 2 volumes of millet, and simmering for 30–35 minutes.
